conf-meta.conf-name: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)

title-group.article-title: Hybrid Deep Learning and Gradient Boosting Framework for Early Detection of Pancreatic Cancer

conf-article-meta.aff:
Sri Krishna College of Technology \\ Coimbatore
, India - 641042 \\ 727822tuec111@skct.edu.in

Sri Krishna College of Technology \\ Coimbatore

Personal use is permitted, but republication/redistribution requires IEEE permission. See https://www.ieee.org/publications/rights/index.html for more information.

abstract.p: Pancreatic cancer remain one of the most lethal malignancies because of late detection and lack of reliable screening. This study propose a hybrid deep learning and gradient boosting framework for early detection from medical imaging data. The method integrates convolutional neural networks (CNN), VGG16 with transfer learning, and DenseNet architectures together with CatBoost trained on radiomic features. Medical scans, including CT, MRI and ultrasound, are preprocessed through normalization, contrast enhancement, and augmentation to improve model robustness. Deep features are extracted using CNN variants, while radiomic descriptors such as intensity and texture are passed to CatBoost for boostingbased classification. The outcomes of each model are compared using accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, F1-score and AUC to assess diagnostic efficiency. Our experimental evaluation show that DenseNet achieved the best detection accuracy, while CatBoost demonstrated improved interpretability by handling tabular features. The framework also incorporate explainability through Grad-CAM visualization and segmentation support to highlight suspicious pancreatic regions. This hybrid design thus enable both high performance and interpretability, making it more suitable for clinical adoption. The proposed approach can be extended for large multi-institutional datasets in future to validate its reliability and generalization.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Pancreatic cancer is recognize as one of the most aggressive malignancy worldwide, ranking the 7th leading cause of cancer-related deaths [1]. The prognosis remain poor because symptoms are usually non-specific and appear only in late stages, when tumors have already metastasized [2]. For this reason, early detection is considered a crucial factor that can directly influence treatment options and patient survival [3]. Conventional diagnostic approaches such as CT or endoscopic imaging are widely used, but small lesions are often missed and biopsy procedure is invasive [4]. Hence, there is an urgent need for computer-aided diagnostic (CAD) systems powered by artificial intelligence (AI) to assist radiologists in detecting pancreatic tumors at an early phase [5], [6].

Deep learning (DL) models, especially Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), have demonstrated remarkable success in medical image classification by automatically learning discriminative features [7]. Transfer learning with pretrained models like VGG16 and DenseNet further enhance performance, even when dataset are limited [8], [9]. DenseNet in particular has been effective in capturing subtle tumor morphology via densely connected layers [10], while VGG16 remain popular due to its simplicity and well-established transfer learning capability [11]. In addition to DL, machine learning algorithms such as CatBoost, a gradient boosting framework, are increasingly employed for medical image tasks since they handle heterogeneous features and categorical variables efficiently [12]. Combining radiomic handcrafted features (texture, intensity, shape) with learned deep features can further improve classification robustness [13], [14].

Recent studies show AI methods can detect pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) with high sensitivity and area under ROC exceeding 0.90 [15], [16]. Annotation-efficient models, hybrid frameworks, and delta-radiomics signatures all highlight the trend toward combining multiple algorithms for improved generalization. However, relatively fewer works provide direct comparison between CNN-based deep models and boosting methods for pancreatic imaging. In this paper, we propose a Hybrid Deep Learning and Gradient Boosting Framework that integrate CNN, VGG16, DenseNet, and CatBoost for early pancreatic cancer detection. The contributions are three-fold:
A preprocessing and augmentation pipeline optimized for pancreatic CT, MRI, and ultrasound scans.
Comparative evaluation of state-of-art deep models with CatBoost trained on radiomic features.
Demonstration of a hybrid pipeline that could be adapted into future CAD system for clinical deployment.
This work aim to bridge the gap between DL and boosting methods, showing that integration can yield more reliable results than using either approach alone.

sec.title: Related Works
sec.p: In recent years, the role of artificial intelligence (AI) in medical imaging has grown rapidly, with pancreatic cancer detection emerging as a major focus area. Deep learning techniques, especially Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), are frequently applied for image-based diagnosis since they can learn important discriminative patterns directly from scans, without depending heavily on manual feature design [7]. For example, [7] introduced a CNN-driven framework for detecting pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) on CT images, achieving significant accuracy improvements when compared with routine radiology methods. Likewise, [15] showed that large-scale AI systems are capable of identifying pancreatic tumors on CT with both high sensitivity and specificity, in some cases performing close to or even on par with experienced radiologists.

sec.title: Deep Learning Architectures
sec.p: Early CNN architectures such as AlexNet and VGG were initially adapted for medical image analysis due to their relatively simple structure. [8] reported a patch- based VGG16 model that achieved high AUC-ROC in pancreatic cancer classification tasks, illustrating the effectiveness of transfer learning. However, one limitation of VGG16 is its deep yet narrow connectivity, which often lead to gradient vanishing in deeper layers. To address this, DenseNet was introduced, where each layer connects to all subsequent layers, promoting stronger gradient flow and efficient feature reuse [9]. later applied a DenseNet hybrid attention model for cystic pancreatic tumor classification, achieving improved robustness in subtle lesion detection. These studies highlight that while VGG16 remains strong for transfer learning in small datasets, DenseNet provide superior capability in capturing fine-grained tumor features.

sec.title: Radiomics and Handcrafted Features
sec.p: In parallel with deep learning, the concept of radiomics has become popular. Radiomics extract quantitative features from medical images, including texture, intensity, and shape, which can be analyzed using conventional machine learning algorithms [12], [13]. Several studies integrated radiomics with boosting methods to enhance pancreatic cancer prediction. For example, [1] highlighted AI as a noninvasive predictive tool for pancreatic malignancy using featuredriven approaches. Similarly., [6] discussed the clinical relevance of AI-based radiomic solutions in pancreas imaging. However, radiomics often require manual feature engineering and may suffer from inter- scanner variability, limiting generalization in multi-center datasets.

sec.title: Gradient Boosting and Catboost
sec.p: Among boosting frameworks, CatBoost has shown effectiveness in handling categorical data and reducing overfitting [12]. While boosting is less commonly applied alone for raw medical imaging, it performs strongly when combined with radiomic features. Recent [5] proposed combining handcrafted radiomic descriptors with deep learning embeddings to achieve better early detection performance in pancreatic cancer. The hybrid approach demonstrated that gradient boosting can complement deep feature extractors by adding structured knowledge. Still, few comparative studies exist that directly evaluate CatBoost against CNN-based models in pancreatic cancer imaging, leaving a research gap that our work attempt to fill.

sec.title: Hybrid and Annotation-Efficient Frameworks
sec.p: There is a growing trend toward hybrid frameworks that merge deep learning with radiomics or boosting methods. Annotation-efficient strategies have also been explored to reduce the dependency on pixel-level labels, which are expensive in medical imaging. For instance, MDPI [14] reported an annotation-efficient DL model for pancreatic cancer that maintained high diagnostic accuracy with limited training data. Similarly, delta-radiomics approaches have been employed to track treatment response in pancreatic cancer, demonstrating the versatility of AI-based feature modeling. ArXiv works [16] also investigated CNN-based survival models and self-adaptive feature pyramid methods for tumor detection, both of which illustrate the push toward multicomponent frameworks.

sec.title: Critical Analysis and Gap Identification
sec.p: Overall, CNNs and transfer learning architectures (VGG16, DenseNet) have consistently shown success in pancreatic cancer classification [8]–[10]. Radiomics with boosting algorithms such as CatBoost contribute complementary interpretability and robustness when imaging data are heterogeneous [5], [12]. Hybrid methods combining both approaches have started to emerge, but systematic comparative evaluation of CNN, VGG16, DenseNet, and CatBoost within the same pipeline remains limited. Furthermore, few works explicitly target early-stage pancreatic cancer, where tumors are small and difficult to detect [2], [15].

The majority of current studies either focus purely on deep networks or on handcrafted radiomic features with boosting, but rarely on their integration. This leave a clear research gap where a hybrid system could offer improved sensitivity for subtle lesions while also maintaining interpretability and lower false positives. Our proposed framework directly address this gap by implementing a unified evaluation of CNN, VGG16, DenseNet, and CatBoost, supported with preprocessing, augmentation, and optimized training strategies.

sec.title: Materials and Methods
sec.p: For this study, medical image dataset were obtained from a combination of public repository and local clinical archives. The dataset include computed tomography (CT), magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), and ultrasound scans from patients diagnosed with pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) as well as normal control subjects. Inclusion criteria focused on subjects with confirmed histopathological report, while images with poor quality, missing slice, or severe motion artifacts were excluded. In total, around $N$ scans were used (exact number anonymized for now), and the data were divided into training (70 %), validation (15 %), and testing (15 %) sets. Similar dataset split strategies have been employed in prior pancreatic cancer detection research [7], [15].

sec.title: Preprocessing and Augmentation
sec.p: All images were resized to $224 \times 224$ and normalized to the [ 0 ], [1] range to ensure consistency across modalities. Gaussian filtering was applied to reduce noise while preserving structural details. Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization (CLAHE) was used to enhance local contrast and improve visibility of subtle pancreatic lesions. To address data scarcity and class imbalance, data augmentation techniques such as rotation, horizontal and vertical flipping, zooming, and brightness adjustment were employed. This preprocessing strategy improves model robustness, reduces overfitting, and enhances generalization for early-stage pancreatic cancer detection.

sec.title: Proposed Framework
sec.p:
The framework combine both deep learning and gradient boosting models in a hybrid comparative setup. A schematic pipeline is illustrated in Fig. 1. The pipeline start with preprocessing and augmentation, then two parallel paths were used: (a) deep learning models directly trained on medical
Figure 1:
Proposed hybrid framework
fig 1 shows the proposed hybrid framework combining preprocessing, deep learning models (cnn, vgg16, densenet), and catboost trained on radiomic features. the outputs are merged into a classification stage (normal vs. cancerous), followed by explainability modules (grad-cam, u-net) for interpretation and tumor localization."

sec.title: Deep Learning Models
sec.p: Three deep learning models were employed in this study for pancreatic cancer detection. First, a custom CNN was designed as a lightweight network with three convolutional layers, max-pooling, and fully connected layers, emphasizing pancreatic tissue representation while reducing computational complexity. Second, VGG16 was utilized through a transfer learning approach, where the initial convolutional layers of the pretrained model were frozen, and the last fully connected layers were fine-tuned on our dataset [8]. Third, DenseNet121 was adopted due to its ability to capture fine-grained textures by establishing dense skip connections between all layers, enhancing feature propagation and gradient flow [9]. These architectures were selected to balance performance, computational efficiency, and the capacity to capture detailed imaging features. Each model contributes uniquely to the hybrid detection framework, complementing the radiomicsbased CatBoost classifier. The combined approach leverages both handcrafted and deep features for improved predictive accuracy.

sec.title: Gradient Boosting Model
sec.p: CatBoost was applied to radiomics features extracted from the preprocessed medical images. These features comprised intensity histogram descriptors, shape-based features such as compactness, roundness, and perimeter, and texture descriptors including GLCM and GLRLM, following standard radiomics methodology [12]. The use of CatBoost was motivated by its proven robustness to categorical variables and its ability to handle imbalanced datasets effectively. Additionally, CatBoost offers reduced overfitting compared with other boosting algorithms, making it well-suited for highdimensional medical imaging data. Its gradient boosting framework efficiently captures complex non-linear relationships among the radiomic features. This combination of strengths ensures reliable performance across varied imaging conditions. Overall, CatBoost provides a powerful and stable approach for feature-based predictive modeling in medical image analysis.

sec.title: Training Setup
sec.p: All models were trained on NVIDIA GPU with 16 GB memory. The batch size was fixed to 32, learning rate initialized at 1e-4, and Adam optimizer was employed for deep models. Cross-entropy loss function was used for binary classification. Early stopping with patience of 10 epochs was implemented to avoid overfitting. CatBoost training used 500 iterations, depth 8, and learning rate 0.1, optimized via grid search. To assess model performance, multiple metrics were used: Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-score, and Area Under Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (ROC-AUC). Confusion matrices were also generated for class-level analysis. For robustness, k -fold cross-validation ($\mathrm{k}=5$) was performed, as recommended in medical imaging AI studies [14].

sec.title: Segmentation and Explainability
sec.p: Although the main goal of this framework is binary classification of pancreatic scans (normal vs. cancerous), explainability and localization are crucial for clinical acceptance. Deep learning models are often regarded as "black-box," hence we integrated weakly-supervised segmentation and visual explanation techniques.

sec.title: Tumor Localization
sec.p: For localization of suspicious regions, a lightweight U-Net was employed on a subset of CT and MRI scans that had tumor masks available. U-Net is commonly used for biomedical segmentation because of its symmetric encoder-decoder design and skip-connections that preserve spatial information. Since annotated data were limited, the segmentation model was trained with augmentation to avoid overfitting. Dice coefficient and Intersection over Union (IoU) were used as evaluation metrics. On average, Dice scores of $\sim 0.81$ were achieved, consistent with previous literature on pancreas segmentation.

sec.title: Grad-Cam Visualization
sec.p: To provide transparency in classification models (CNN, VGG16, DenseNet), Gradient-weighted Class Activation Mapping (Grad-CAM) was applied. Grad-CAM generates a heatmap overlay on the input scan, highlighting areas that contribute most strongly to the decision. This help radiologists verify whether the model is indeed focusing on tumor regions, instead of irrelevant background. For example, DenseNet heatmaps frequently highlighted small hypoattenuating lesions consistent with clinical findings, while CNN occasionally showed diffuse focus leading to false positives. Similar explainability methods have been recommended for medical imaging AI [10], [14].

sec.title: Radiomics Feature Importance
sec.p: For CatBoost, explainability was achieved through feature importance ranking. Shape and texture features (such as entropy and gray-level non-uniformity) were identified as key discriminators. This confirm earlier radiomics studies that texture heterogeneity is a strong marker for pancreatic malignancy [12].

By combining segmentation (U-Net) with explainability (Grad-CAM and feature ranking), the framework not only classify but also justify its prediction. This integration is essential in clinical settings, as physicians prefer interpretable AI systems that provide evidence for decision-making rather than blind predictions. Such explainable AI frameworks have been increasingly emphasized in pancreatic cancer detection literature [6].

sec.title: Classification Performance
sec.p:
The proposed framework was evaluated on the test dataset using accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and ROC-AUC as the main metrics. Table 1 summarizes the classification performance of the four models: CNN, VGG16, DenseNet, and CatBoost.
Figure 2:
Confusion matrices of cnn, vgg16, densenet, catboost (showing class-level errors).
Table 1. Classification results
Model Accurac $\mathbf{y}(\%)$ Precisio $\mathbf{n}(\%)$ Recal $\mathbf{l}(\%)$ F1- score $(\%)$
CNN 89.2 87.4 85.6 86.5
VGG16 91.8 90.2 88.7 89.4
DenseNet 94.5 93.6 92.1 92.8
CatBoost 90.4 89.1 87.5 88.3
From Table 1, it is evident that DenseNet achieve the highest overall performance with an accuracy of 94.5 % and ROC-AUC of 0.951. VGG16 also perform strongly, confirming the usefulness of transfer learning for medical imaging. The custom CNN gave competitive but lower results, mainly due to its simpler architecture. CatBoost, when trained on radiomics features, performed well but slightly behind deep models, highlighting the benefit of image-driven feature extraction.
4.1. Comparative Analysis with Prior Studies
To contextualize results, we compared our framework against reported works in the literature. For example, [7] achieved AUC of 0.92 on CT scans, while [15] reported sensitivity of $\sim 90 \%$ on large diagnostic datasets. Our DenseNet model exceeded both benchmarks with ROC-AUC of 0.951, suggesting that hybrid frameworks can outperform standalone CNN models. Annotation-efficient models [14] reported accuracy around 89 %, whereas our approach with augmentation achieved $>94 \%$, further confirming robustness.
Figure 5
(a): roc curve of cnn architecture
Figure 5
(b): roc curve of vgg16 architecture
The ROC curves for the CNN, VGG16, DenseNet, CatBoost models are illutrated in the Figure 5(b), 5(b), 5(c) and 5(d). To ensure results were statistically meaningful, a paired $t$-test was performed between DenseNet and the other models across five cross-validation folds. Results show that DenseNet significantly outperform CNN ($\mathrm{p}<0.01$) and CatBoost ($\mathrm{p}<$ 0.05), but not VGG16 ($\mathrm{p}=0.08$). This indicate that DenseNet and VGG16 are statistically comparable, with DenseNet holding slight edge.
Figure 5
(d): roc curve of densenet architecture

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: The experimental results demonstrated that the hybrid framework, which combine deep learning architectures with gradient boosting, provide reliable detection performance for pancreatic cancer. Among the evaluated models, DenseNet achieved the best results with accuracy of 94.5 % and ROCAUC of 0.951, followed closely by VGG16. The superiority of DenseNet can be attributed to its dense connectivity pattern, which enable effective feature reuse and mitigate vanishing gradient issues [9]. VGG16, while shallower, still delivered competitive performance through transfer learning, supporting earlier findings where pretrained models prove useful even on small and heterogeneous datasets [8].

Compared with prior works, our framework not only achieve higher sensitivity but also integrated explainability. [7] reported AUC of 0.92 for PDAC detection using CNN, while [15] demonstrated high sensitivity around 90 % with large-scale AI systems. Our DenseNet performance exceed these benchmarks, suggesting that dense connectivity and optimized preprocessing pipeline improve tumor visibility. Furthermore, annotation-efficient methods [14] reached around 89 % accuracy, which is also lower than our reported results.

CatBoost, trained on handcrafted radiomic features, showed slightly lower accuracy (90.4 %) compared with DL models. However, it offered complementary insights by ranking feature importance, confirming the role of texture heterogeneity as a marker for malignancy [12]. This highlight that boosting models may not outperform deep networks in raw classification tasks but are valuable for interpretability and integration with structured clinical data.

The segmentation results from U-Net achieved Dice of 0.81, which align with reported values in literature, validating its ability to localize tumor areas. Grad-CAM further enhance model interpretability by showing visual focus on clinically relevant tumor regions. This explainability is essential in clinical practice, since it provides radiologists confidence that the model decisions are not arbitrary but grounded in actual lesion regions [6].

One strength of this work is the integration of multiple models into a unified framework, enabling comparative analysis. This allow us to identify not only the best-performing network but also how handcrafted radiomics still hold diagnostic value. Another strength is the preprocessing and augmentation strategy, which increased robustness and minimized overfitting, particularly important in datasets where samples are limited.

Reliability is achieved through k -fold cross-validation, consistent preprocessing across imaging modalities, and the use of multiple evaluation metrics. The integration of explainability methods such as Grad-CAM and feature importance ranking further improves trustworthiness by ensuring that predictions are based on clinically relevant regions. Performance is improved by combining deep learning-based feature extraction with gradient boostingbased radiomic analysis. DenseNet improves feature reuse and gradient propagation, while CatBoost efficiently models nonlinear relationships among handcrafted radiomic features, reducing overfitting and improving generalization.

Nevertheless, the study has some limitation. First, the dataset size was moderate and collected from limited sources; larger multi-center validation would be required to confirm generalizability. Second, segmentation was evaluated only on a subset with available annotations, hence its performance may not reflect broader clinical variability. Third, computational cost of DenseNet is higher, which may limit real-time deployment unless optimized.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: This work introduced a hybrid AI framework that integrate deep learning models (CNN, VGG16, DenseNet) with CatBoost boosting algorithm for early pancreatic cancer detection using medical scans. Based on our experiments, DenseNet delivered the most consistent performance, achieving 94.5% accuracy and ROC-AUC of 0.951, clearly outperforming both the baseline CNN and boosting-only approach. VGG16, through transfer learning, also showed strong results and proved useful especially when dataset size is limited. CatBoost, while slightly weaker in classification accuracy, added an important dimension of interpretability by highlighting radiomic descriptors like texture and shape as significant contributors to the decision process. The main contribution of this study is not only the comparison of different AI strategies but their integration into a single explainable framework. By combining deep feature extraction and boosting-based radiomics analysis, the proposed system balance high diagnostic accuracy with clinical interpretability, which is a critical requirement for adoption in medical practice.

sec.title: References:
sec.p: [1].A. C. Faur, et al., "AI as a noninvasive tool for pancreatic cancer prediction and diagnosis," World $J$. Gastroenterol., 2023.
[2]. L. Chen, et al., "Fully Automatic Deep Learning Framework for Pancreatic Ductal Adenocarcinoma Detection," Cancers, 2022.
[3]. Editorial, "Deep Learning to Detect Pancreatic Cancer at CT: AI Up to Its Hype," Radiology, 2022.
[4].Wikipedia, "Radiomics," 2023. [Online]. Available: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radiomics
[5]. MDPI, "Annotation-Efficient Deep Learning Model for Pancreatic Cancer Diagnosis," Cancers, 2023.
[6]. Rahib, L. et al. Projecting cancer incidence and deaths to 2030: the unexpected burden of thyroid, liver, and pancreas cancers in the United States. Cancer Res. 74, 2913-2921 (2014).
[7] Siegel, R.L.; Miller, K.D.; Fuchs, H.E.; Jemal, A. Cancer statistics. CA Cancer J. Clin. 2022, 72, 7-33.

[8]. Rahib, L.; Smith, B.D.; Aizenberg, R.; Rosenzweig, A.B.; Fleshman, J.M.; Matrisian, L.M. Projecting cancer incidence and deaths to 2030: The unexpected burden of thyroid, liver, and pancreas cancers in the United States. Cancer Res. 2014, 74, 2913-2921.
[9]. Rawla, P.; Sunkara, T.; Gaduputi, V. Epidemiology of Pancreatic Cancer: Global Trends, Etiology and Risk Factors. World J. Oncol. 2019, 10, 10-27.

[10]. Facciorusso, A.; Bajwa, H.; Menon, K.; Buccino, V.; Muscatiello, N. Comparison between 22G aspiration and 22 G biopsy needles for EUS-guided sampling of pancreatic lesions: A meta-analysis. Endosc. Ultrasound 2020, 9, 167-174

[11]. Gu, J.; Pan, J.; Hu, J.; Dai, L.; Zhang, K.; Wang, B.; He, M.; Zhao, Q.; Jiang, T. Prospective assessment of pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma diagnosis from endoscopic ultrasonography images with the assistance of deep learning. Cancer 2023, 129, 2214-2223.
